cataloging_subject.label: Three Prose Pieces from Ratis Raving and Other Moral and Religious Pieces
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the exact three-work electronic selection represented by RatisRav: Craft of Deyng, Dicta Salomonis (headed Wisdom of Solomon by Lumby), and The Vertewis of the Mess. It is not one medieval work, the eponymous Ratis Raving, all eleven works in Cambridge University Library MS Kk.1.5 part 6, or Lumby's complete 1870/Greenwood 1969 volume.

catalog_summary.title: Three Prose Pieces from Ratis Raving and Other Moral and Religious Pieces
catalog_summary.author: Three anonymous Older Scots translators or compilers
catalog_summary.editor: J. Rawson Lumby; three-piece electronic selection by the University of Michigan Humanities Text Initiative
catalog_summary.date: Individual works composed in the fifteenth century; copied circa 1480-1490; first printed in 1870; exact electronic selection published in 1997
catalog_summary.region: Lowland Scotland / Older Scots; exact composition places unresolved

titles.value: Three Prose Pieces from Ratis Raving and Other Moral and Religious Pieces
notes: The supplied title makes the three-work boundary explicit and avoids implying that the eponymous Ratis Raving is included.

titles.value: Ratis Raving, and Other Moral and Religious Pieces, in Prose and Verse
notes: Exact electronic-item title inherited from the broader source volume; it overstates the encoded contents if read without the selection note.
The manuscript has no independent title rubric; the title derives from the opening's description of the treatise.
Lumby's printed and encoded heading; the manuscript explicit supplies Dicta Salomonis.
The brackets in Lumby's heading mark the title as editorially supplied from the opening words.

agents.name: Anonymous creators of the three Older Scots prose works
notes: A discovery collective only; it does not assert common authorship or one act of composition.

notes: Prepared the complete manuscript copy used in producing Lumby's edition.

scope.description: Anonymous translation and abridging adaptation of Ars Moriendi material

notes: Richard Rolle and Jean Gerson are excluded as authors; Gerson is an internal chronological reference.

scope.description: Anonymous Older Scots paraphraser or translator of Ecclesiastes

notes: Girvan argues for an unidentified French intermediary. Solomon is the represented biblical speaker, not the medieval translator; V. de F. is the witness scribe.

scope.description: Anonymous compilation of sayings on the benefits of hearing Mass

notes: The saints named as authorities and John Lydgate are not assigned authorship of this prose tract.

form_statements.description: All three selected works are prose; the XML BODY contains 24 P elements (1, 1, and 22 by component) and no L elements. Eight additional P elements occur in HEADER metadata, for 32 P elements file-wide.

subject_statements.label: Purgatory, masses, and prayer for the dead

summaries.value: A bounded electronic selection of three independently composed fifteenth-century Older Scots prose works: an abridged art-of-dying treatise, a paraphrase of Ecclesiastes, and a compilation of attributed sayings on the spiritual and temporal benefits of hearing Mass.
The XML retains Lumby's item numbers 1, 6, and 11. The gaps are deliberate: U-M selected only the three prose pieces and excluded the eight verse pieces, including the eponymous Ratis Raving.
An anonymous Older Scots abridgment of the Latin Ars Moriendi tradition, treating the commendation of death, preparation for dying, faith, repentance, restitution, forgiveness, and final prayer.
An anonymous prose paraphrase of Ecclesiastes on vanity, wealth, labor, wisdom, mortality, judgment, and service to God, probably mediated by an unidentified French translation.
An anonymous compilation of sayings attributed to Christ, Paul, and patristic authorities about hearing Mass, including benefits for prayer, health, childbirth, purgatory, protection, and salvation.

Authorship, date, sources, and genre are scoped to the three component works. Print publication, manuscript copying, digital selection, and encoding are distinct events and are not inherited as common composition facts.

extent.description: The repository XML BODY contains three top-level DIV1 units numbered 1, 6, and 11, with 24 P elements distributed 1, 1, and 22 by component, and no L elements. Eight additional P elements occur in HEADER metadata, making 32 P elements file-wide. Paragraph elements are representation metrics, not intellectual item counts; the source manuscripts write the first two works as solid prose. The selection is complete relative to U-M's declared prose-only boundary.

content_parts.label: Craft of Deyng
content_parts.summary: Anonymous Older Scots abridging translation of Ars Moriendi material.

locators.value: pp. 1-8; PDF pp. 19-26; IA viewer n18-n25

notes: The XML represents the work with one P element; that encoding choice is not a claim that the work has one intellectual paragraph.

content_parts.label: Dicta Salomonis (Wisdom of Solomon)
content_parts.summary: Anonymous Older Scots prose paraphrase of Ecclesiastes, probably through an unidentified French intermediary.

locators.value: pp. 11-25; PDF pp. 29-43; IA viewer n28-n42

notes: The explicit per manum V. de F. identifies the witness scribe's hand, not authorship of the work.

content_parts.label: The Vertewis of the Mess
content_parts.summary: Anonymous prose collection of patristic and scriptural sayings about the virtues of hearing Mass.

locators.value: pp. 113-115; PDF pp. 131-133; IA viewer n130-n132

related_works.title: Ars Moriendi
notes: Applies only to Craft of Deyng. Girvan describes an independent abridging translation of part of the Latin tradition; no exact physical Latin exemplar is identified.

related_works.title: Ecclesiastes
notes: Applies only to Dicta Salomonis. The immediate source was probably an unidentified French translation rather than the Vulgate directly.

related_works.title: The Vertue of the Masse
notes: A verse analogue to The Vertewis of the Mess printed by Wynkyn de Worde about 1520 and attributed to John Lydgate; no direct-descent relationship is asserted.

evidence.citation: General Corpus pinned CME source XML, RatisRav.xml.
evidence.summary: Establishes the exact 1997 selection, electronic title, source carrier statement, production workflow, three headings, text, printed-page milestones, manuscript-folio milestones, and restrictive legacy availability statement.

evidence.citation: Direct structural inspection of the pinned RatisRav XML.
evidence.summary: Confirms 60,783 bytes; three BODY DIV1 units; 24 BODY P elements distributed 1, 1, and 22 by component; eight HEADER P elements and therefore 32 P elements file-wide; no L elements; and the component page and folio milestone ranges.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, RatisRav table of contents.
evidence.summary: Public item view for the electronic text and its three selected divisions.

evidence.citation: J. Rawson Lumby, ed., Ratis Raving, and Other Moral and Religious Pieces in Prose and Verse, EETS OS 43 (London: N. Trübner & Co., 1870).
evidence.locator: Preface and notes; Craft pp. 1-8; Wisdom pp. 11-25; Vertewis pp. 113-115
evidence.summary: Establishes Lumby's title, editorial responsibility, King's manuscript copy, print locators, manuscript folios, Ff.5.45 comparison, and Wynkyn de Worde analogue. The cached 528-page provider object is a compound bound scan containing the complete 1870 edition plus additional volumes.

evidence.citation: R. Girvan, ed., Ratis Raving and Other Early Scots Poems on Morals, Scottish Text Society, 3rd series 11 (Edinburgh and London: William Blackwood & Sons, 1939).
evidence.locator: Introduction and source discussion; Appendix pp. 166-174, 177-194
evidence.summary: Provides the current component-level authorship cautions, approximate dates, source relations, manuscript-part history, scribe analysis, and complete later editions of the three prose works.
notes: The provider PDF is structurally readable and all target pages render; qpdf reports non-fatal linearization hint-table warnings, so the original provider bytes were preserved without repair.

evidence.citation: M. R. James, unpublished description of Cambridge University Library MS Kk.1.5, transcription prepared by Martin Blake, published by Cambridge University Library (2021).
evidence.summary: Official CUL publication of the historical description, confirming the composite codex, separately covered part 6, physical extent, and exact folios of the three works.

evidence.citation: Helsinki Corpus of Older Scots bibliography and text classification entries for Craft of Deyng, Dicta Salomonis, and The Vertewis of the Mess.
evidence.summary: Confirms Girvan page-and-line ranges, corpus word counts, Older Scots classification, and the explicitly doubtful status of the 1460 date assigned to Dicta Salomonis.

evidence.citation: RatisRav lineage evidence for the Greenwood-Lumby-King-Kk.1.5 carrier chain.
evidence.summary: Supports the distinction among the 1969 source copy, 1870 edition, working transcript, manuscript part, digital encoding, and repository artifact.

evidence.citation: RatisRav lineage evidence for the Ff.5.45 and Wynkyn de Worde supporting analogues.
evidence.summary: Supports analogue-only relations without making either source an ancestor of the selected Kk.1.5 texts.

open_questions.question: What exact Latin exemplar and textual relationship underlie Craft of Deyng and its fuller Middle English analogues in CUL MS Ff.5.45 and Bodleian MS Douce 322?
next_steps: Collate the Scots work against complete texts of the Latin Ars Moriendi family and the two English analogues; do not infer direct descent from opening correspondence alone.

open_questions.question: Can the unidentified French intermediary proposed for Dicta Salomonis be identified, and can the identity of the scribe V. de F. be established?
next_steps: Compare the paraphrase against medieval French Ecclesiastes traditions; retain V. de F. as an unidentified scribe, not an author.

open_questions.question: Can the immediate source collection for The Vertewis of the Mess be identified?
next_steps: Collate its sayings against Simmons's parallels, Reliquiae Antiquae, and the Lydgate/de Worde tradition while preserving the current analogue-only relation.

open_questions.question: Can a complete lawful digital facsimile of Cambridge University Library MS Kk.1.5 be obtained?
next_steps: Use CUL's official imaging order route for the whole present codex if complete physical coverage is required; recheck CUDL periodically.

notes: The preferred title is intentionally supplied and boundary-explicit because the electronic item does not contain the eponymous Ratis Raving.
The three works have independent authorship, dates, genres, and source relations; manuscript juxtaposition and electronic selection do not create common medieval authorship.
Solomon, Gerson, Rolle, cited saints, and V. de F. are not cataloged as authors of the three works.

The current CUL host codex, its historically independent part 6, King's working transcript, Lumby's edition, Greenwood's reprint, U-M's encoding, and the repository XML remain distinct lineage layers.
Rights, provider terms, access, cache integrity, and dated no-public-facsimile findings are recorded in the companion lineage manifest.
